content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Tava Ostrenger 
Assistant City Attorney 
City of Santa Barbara 
PO Box 1990 
Santa Barbara, CA 93102 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-088 
Dear Ms. Ostrenger: 
January 6, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Barbara City Golf Course 
Superintendent Simon Herrera regarding Government Code Section l 090, et seq. 1 Please note that 
we do not advise on any other area oflaw, other than Section l 090 and the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act").2 We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice, and any advice we provide 
assumes your facts are complete and accurate.3 Moreover, the Commission does not advise with 
respect to past conduct.4 Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate 
any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply 
only to prospective actions. 
As required by statute, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and 
the Santa Barbara County District Attorney's Office and we did not receive a written response from 
either entity.5 Finally, we are required to advise you that the following advice is not admissible in a 
criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. 6 
1 Government Code Sections l090 through 1097.5. All statutory references arc to the Government Code, 
unless otherwise indicated. 
2 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9!014. The regulations 
of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California 
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code ofRegulatiqns, unless 
otherwise indicated. 
3 In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71. 
4 Section 109 l.7(c)(2). 
s Section 1097. l(c)(3) and (4). 
6 Section 1097.l(c)(S). 


QUESTIONS 
File No. A-16-088 
Page No. 2 
1. Does Superintendent Herrera's involvement in the City's Request For Proposal ("RFP") 
to outsource management of the City's golf course preclude him under Section 1090 from leaving 
city employment and seeking private employment with the selected company, CourseCo, to provide 
services he is currently performing as the City's Golf Course Superintendent? 
2. Under Section 1090, may Superintendent Herrera leave city employment and seek private 
employment as the Superintendent with the selected company, CourseCo, at a course not owned by 
the City of Santa Barbara? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Yes. Superintendent Herrera cannot be hired by CourseCo as the City's Golf Course 
Superintendent without violating Section 1090. This is consistent with the City's representations to 
Superintendent Herrera and its agreement with CourseCo that he could not be hired in that position 
because he participated in making the contract. 
2. Yes, so long as he did not have an understanding, agreement, or arrangement regarding 
prospective employment with CourseCo at the time he participated in making the contract with the 
company. 
FACTS 
The Santa Barbara Municipal Golf Course ("Golf Course") provides golf and recreational 
opportunities to the City of Santa Barbara (the "City") residents and visitors. The Golf Course has 
seen play decline since 1990, and has been challenged by competitive pricing trends in the local 
golf market. 
Mr. Herrera began employment with the City as Maintenance Supervisor II in February 
2013, and has held that position continuously since then. Mr. Herrera is assigned exclusively to the 
Golf Course as the Maintenance Supervisor, and he is a Certified Golf Course Superintendent 
through the Golf Course Superintendents of America. He is referred to informally by the City as the 
Golf Course Superintendent. As part of his duties, Mr. Herrera was responsible for overseeing 
general maintenance of the Golf Course, including maintenance of the greens, driving range, 
clubhouse, parking lot, Golf Course equipment, purchasing materials, and overseeing renovation 
projects. Mr. Herrera supervised a crew of21 City employees to assist in overseeing the 
maintenance of the Golf Course. 
On March 10, 2015, the City Council prioritized the need for the Golf Course to remain a 
self-supporting enterprise not dependent upon the General Fund, and directed the Parks & 
Recreation Department to move forward with contracting out management of the Golf Course to 
reduce on-going golf expenses. 
On July 28, 2015, Council subsequently directed staff to proceed with the RFP for 
professional golf management services, including golf course management, Pro Shop, and course 


File No. A-16-088 
Page No. 3 
maintenance. With this direction, the Parks & Recreation Director created a team of City employees 
to assist in drafting the RFP and final selection of the management contractor. 
Once the contractor was selected and a new contract took effect, the City Golf Course would 
be managed by the selected company and its employees. Because Superintendent Herrera was 
assigned exclusively to the Golf Course as a Maintenance Supervisor, his position was eliminated, 
as well as those of other City employees who worked at the City's Golf Course, when the private 
firm took over the maintenance. Affected City employees could either be terminated, transferred to 
other city parks jobs, or obtain employment with the private firm hired to perform the contract 
services. 
The Santa Barbara Golf Course RFP included a "staffing plan" that encouraged retention of 
and "continued employment" of city employees by the winning contract bidder at the Santa Barbara 
Golf Club. 
This was an "evaluating factor" that the city considered in ranking the bid proposals - the 
"extent to which the respondent proposal includes a plan to retain any existing staff." The RFP 
states, "Although not mandatory, it is highly desirable to offer existing staff (maintenance and 
Proshop) the opportunity for continued employment at the Santa Barbara Golf Club."7 
Because Mr. Herrera was intimately involved in the drafting of the RFP, Santa Barbara 
Parks and Recreation Manager Mark Sewell stated that he had verbally made clear to all potential 
bidders that Mr. Herrera was "unavailable to be transferred to an incoming company given his 
involvement in the maintenance standard setting in the RFP" and that he "could not take a role with 
the new contractor.''8 
The individuals assigned to the team to draft the RFP and help select the management 
contractor were the Assistant City Administrator, Assistant City Attorney, the Parks and Recreation 
Business Manager, the Finance Department Director, the General Services Manager, the City's 
Independent Operations Consultant, and Superintendent Herrera. 
7 "City of Santa Barbara Management and Operations Request for Proposals (RFP #3802) for Santa Barbara 
Golf Club," August 27, 2015, page 8. 
8 Santa Barbara Parks and Recreation Manager Marie Sewell stated in an October 19, 2016 email that he 
"originally confirmed in a conversation with CourseCo management, as per the RFP, that Simon [Herrera] was 
unavailable to be transferred to an incoming company given his involvement in the maintenance standard setting in the 
RFP. The RFP encouraged retention of all City employees, with the exception of Simon. Upon hearing from [the City 
Attorney's Office] that Simon may not have a conflict and we could seek clarification from the FPPC, I advised 
CourseCo that would be seeking that advice from the FPPC. I stated that unless and until we heard back that 
[CourseCo] continue to look externally for a Superintendent. Prior to commencement of the CourseCo operations, 
Simon [Herrera] successfully interviewed and was appointed into the position of Parks Supervisor within the Parks 
Division." 
Mr. Sewell later clarified in an October 25, 2016 email that "the RFP document itself was silent on the 
recruitment of Simon [Herrera]" but that "[t)he RFP team had spoken at length internally and I recall stating at the 
mandatory bid walk to all potential proposers that Simon [Herrera] was not to be included in the staffing plan with their 
proposal. It definitely came up in the interviews for the final four proposers, and it was made clear to all proposers 
(including CourseCo) that Simon [Herrera] could not take a role with the new contractor following advice from the City 
Attorney's office." 


File No. A-16-088 
Page No. 4 
Mr. Herrera's involvement with the RFP process and final selection of a maintenance 
company included: reviewing and refining the Golf Course Maintenance Standards that were 
provided as an attachment to the RFP issued in August 2015; participating in the interviews with 
the five management companies selected for consideration; and attending a contract negotiation 
meeting between the selected contractor and the City. 
Mr. Herrera's specific participation in these three events included: 
a. Preparation of RFP Minimum Maintenance Standards 
Mr. Herrera drafted the Maintenance Standards that was attached to the RFP. The 
Maintenance Standards provide the minimum requirements for maintenance of the greens, bunkers, 
irrigation, equipment, and facilities that the City expected the selected management company to 
assume at the Golf Course. 
b. lnten1iew of five finalists 
Mr. Herrera was a member of the Evaluation Committee, and took part in interviewing and 
selecting one of the five golf course management companies that made it through the initial 
selection process. Based on the rankings presented by the Evaluation Committee, the decision was 
made to recommend that the Santa Barbara City Council award the management contract to 
CourseCo. Mr. Herrera did not participate in making the final selection for recommendation to 
Council. 
c. Contract Negotiations 
Once CourseCo was selected as the preferred management company, contract negotiations 
were led by the Parks and Recreation Business Manager, with assistance from the City Attorney's 
Office and the Finance Director. The maintenance requirements drafted by Mr. Herrera, and which 
had been attached to the Request for Proposal, were accepted by CourseCo. Mr. Herrera attended 
one negotiation meeting. 
CourseCo Employment Opportunity 
CourseCo is advertising, interviewing, and hiring a full time employee to act as the Golf 
Course Superintendent for the City's Golf Course. You state that the application processes and 
selection will be made by CourseCo in the open and competitive employment market. 
Although it was made clear to CourseCo staff that Mr. Herrera was not available to be 
transferred and could not take a role with CourseCo per the City's verbal agreement with the 
company, Mr. Herrera stated that he was approached by CourseCo staff about employment there 


File No. A-16-088 
Page No. 5 
"after the contract was awarded." At the time, Mr. Herrera told CourseCo staff, "I preferred to 
continue to work for the City."9 
However, Mr. Herrera is now interested in knowing ifhe can seek employment with 
CourseCo and seeks an opinion from the FPPC as to whether or not his involvement in the RFP 
process would preclude him from becoming employed with CourseCo at the Golf Course, and 
whether such involvement would also preclude employment with CourseCo at any other golf course 
managed by CourseCo but not owned by the City. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The purpose of Section 1090 is to 
ensure that "every public officer be guided solely by the public interest, rather than by personal 
interest, when dealing with contracts in an official capacity." 10 Eliminating temptation for public 
officials, avoiding the perception of impropriety, and obtaining public officials' undivided loyalty 
have been deemed extremely important public policy goals. Therefore, the prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. 11 A contract that 
violates Section 1090 is void.12 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. These steps include: (1) Does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) Does the decision involve a contract; (3) Is the official making or participating in 
making a contract; (4) Does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or 
non-interest exception apply. 
Steps one and two are not at issue here. Your facts indicate that Superintendent Herrera, as 
the City's Golf Course Superintendent is a city employee and the decision at issue involves a 
contract between the City and a private company, CourseCo. Therefore, we focus on the remaining 
steps, including whether Superintendent Herrera has participated in making the contract, whether he 
has a financial interest in the City's contract with CourseCo. to operate its municipal golf course, 
and whether a remote or non-interest exception applies. 
9 Mr. Herrera told the City Attorney in an October 19, 2016 email, "After the contract was awarded to 
CourseCo, one ofCourseCo's staff members inquired as to my availability, and my response was that I preferred to 
continue to work for the City." 
10 Thomson, .. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646. 
11 Id. at p. 648. 
12 Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569. 


File No. A-16-088 
Page No. 6 
Steps 3 and 4: Did Superintendent Herrera make or participate in making a contract 
in which he had a financial interest? 
It is important to note that Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who actually execute 
contracts. Section 1090 casts a wide net to capture those officials who participate in any way in the 
making of the contract. 
The decisional law holds that an official (or a public employee) may be convicted of 
violation even if the official did not actually participate in the execution of the questioned contract, 
if it is established that he had the opportunity to, and did influence execution directly or indirectly 
to promote his personal interests.13 
Therefore, participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving 
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and 
specifications, and solicitation for bids.14 Individuals in advisory positions can influence the 
development of a contract during these early stages of the contracting process even though they 
have no actual power to execute the final contract.15 
Superintendent Herrera has been a long-time supervisor with the City who was assigned 
exclusively to the Golf Course as the Maintenance Supervisor. In that role, he was responsible for 
overseeing general maintenance of the Golf Course, including maintenance of the greens, driving 
range, clubhouse, parking lot, Golf Course equipment, purchasing materials, and overseeing 
renovation projects. 
Superintendent Herrera drafted the Maintenance Standards that were attached to the RFP. 
He was a member of the Evaluation Committee, took part in interviewing and selecting one of the 
five golf course management companies that made it through the initial selection process. 
Additionally, Mr. Herrera attended one negotiation meeting between the City and CourseCo. 
Based on these facts, we conclude that Superintendent Herrera participated in the contract 
between the City and CourseCo. to operate the City's Municipal Golf Course because he was 
involved in drafting Maintenance Standards that were incorporated in the contract, and he 
participated in preliminary discussions, negotiations, planning, solicitation for bids and the ranking 
of candidates. 
13 People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052. 
14 Millbrae Assn. for Residential Sun•im/ v. City of Millbrae ( 1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall 
v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569. 
15 See, e.g., Schaefer v. Berinstein, 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291 ;City Council v. McKinley ( 1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 
204 [member of Park and Recreation Board who owned a landscape architectural finn participated in the making of a 
contract in violation of Section 1090 where he was also a member of a committee created to advise the Board on the 
design, architecture, landscaping and technical planning of a Japanese garden]; see also 81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317, 318-
19 ( 1998) [after leaving office and establishing a private business, Section 1090 precluded former council member from 
participating in City's loan program where he had been involved in the "planning, discussions, and approval necessary 
to implement [the] loan program" and "had the opportunity and did participate in the policy decision 10 create the 
government program under which the contract would later be executed." 


File No. A-16-088 
Page No. 7 
The next issue is whether Superintendent Herrera had a financial interest in and stood to 
financially benefit from the contract between the City and CourseCo. 
Although Section l 090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law 
and Attorney General opinions indicate that the term is liberally interpreted. 16 Moreover, the 
certainty of financial gain is not necessa,y to create a conflict of interest. "The government's right 
to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is diminished as effectively where the 
officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he acts with certainty."17 
The prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve 
financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain.18 "Put in 
ordinary, but nonetheless precise, terms, an official has a financial interest in a contract if he might 
profit from it." (Eden Township Healthcare District v. Sutter Heath (201 1) 202 Cal.App.4th 208, at 
p. 221, quoting People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 at p. 333, italics in original.) 
In addition, courts and the Attorney General have stated that although an official or 
employee may resign from his or her position, that resignation may not be sufficient to avoid a 
Section 1090 violation when that person has been involved in the contracting process. For instance, 
county employees were advised that they could not propose an agreement for consultant services, 
then resign, and provide such consulting services. 19 
The Commission has also issued advice under Section 1090 regarding financial interests 
involving prospective employment. In the Culp Advice Letter, 1-14-051, we advised the Chief 
Deputy Director of a state agency that she had a financial interest in state contracts with various 
technology companies that she approved, if she had an agreement with, or had negotiated to provide 
future consulting services to the companies, or if certain clients of her consulting business were 
named as subcontractors or would otherwise receive benefits from the contracts. 
In Superintendent Herrera's situation, it is clear he participated in creating the contract that 
CourseCo. was ultimately selected to perform. However, at the time Superintendent Herrera 
participated in making the contract, the City made it clear to him and CourseCo. that he could not 
be hired as the City's Golf Course Superintendent. Therefore, he did not stand to financially benefit 
from his participation in the contract even though he created specifications for the RFP, and had the 
discretion to tailor requirements, including specific skill sets, and expertise of the ideal candidate. 
lf he is allowed to seek private employment with CourseCo as the City's Golf Course 
Superintendent, those facts would change the analysis and he would have had a financial interest in 
the contract in violation of Section 1090. 
16 People v. Deysher ( l 934) 2 Cal.2d l 41, 146 ["However devious and winding the chain may be which 
connects the officer with the forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void."] 
11 People ,,. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.41h 1271, 1298 (citations omitted). 
18 Thomson v. Call. supra, 38 Cal.3d 633, 651; see also People v. Va/lerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fu. 
5; Terry ,,. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-432; 85 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001). 
19 66 Ops. Ca.Atty.Gen. 156 (1983). 


File No. A-16-088 
Page No. 8 
This is the type of conduct Section 1090 attempts to thwart, and we recognize the enduring 
policies underlying Section I 090 that "no man can serve two masters," and that officials making 
contracts must not be distracted by personal financial gain but give absolute loyalty and undivided 
allegiance to the best interest of the agency they serve.20 
Given the specific facts in the matter before us, we believe the policies underlying Section 
1090 - eliminating temptation for public officials, avoiding the perception of impropriety, and 
obtaining public officials' undivided loyalty - were meant to prohibit this type of arrangement. 
Therefore, the prohibitions under Section 1090 apply because Superintendent Herrera had a 
financial interest in and stood to personally benefit from the contract in which he assisted the City 
in developing. 
We now tum to the question of whether Mr. Herrera could accept a position with CourseCo 
at a course not owned by the City of Santa Barbara, a position that is not specified in the actual 
contract in question. 
Your facts indicate that there was an understanding or agreement with CourseCo to retain 
city employees after being awarded the contract with the city. This was a factor in the winning bid. 
However, you state that the city had a verbal agreement with CourseCo that Mr. Herrera's 
employment was not part of this arrangement. 
Mr. Herrera also stated that he did not have any understanding, agreement, or arrangement 
with CourseCo regarding prospective employment at the time he participated in making the city's 
contract with CourseCo. He stated he was nevertheless approached by CourseCo staff about 
employment there but only "[a]fter the contract was awarded." 
Any arrangement or agreement made with CourseCo that would financially benefit Mr. 
Herrera at the time the city contract was made-including future employment at an another golf 
course-would be a violation of Section 1090 and Section 87407.21 
However, your facts state that there were no financial incentives or any separate 
arrangement regarding employment that was provided by CourseCo to Mr. Herrera at the time he 
participated in making the city's contract with CourseCo. 
Therefore, Mr. Herrera may pursue employment with CourseCo at a course not owned by 
the City of Santa Barbara so long as he did not have an understanding, agreement, or arrangement 
regarding prospective employment with CourseCo. 
20 See, e.g., Stigall v. Taft, supra, 58 Cal.2d 565, 569; City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 
19 l, l 96; City Council v. McKinley, supra, 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 212. 
11 Section 87407 states that "No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official 
position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom he or she is negotiating, or 
has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment," 
See also People v, Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal. App. 3d 847 [136 Cal. Rptr. 429). The Vallerga case demonstrates 
that Section I 090 applies even when a public official's financial interest flows from a source that is independent of a 
public contract. 


File No. A-16-088 
Page No. 9 
Step Five: Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply? 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where 
the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if ( 1) the officer in question 
discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest is noted 
in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of 
the contract.22 If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the officer's 
abstention, and generally, a noninterest does not require disclosure.23 You have provided no facts to 
suggest that any of the exceptions would apply to this situation. 
Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply? 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" applies to allow making a contract that 
Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit.24 Under the rule of necessity, a government agency may 
acquire an essential service, despite the existence of a conflict, when no source other than that 
which triggers the contract is available; the rule "ensures that essential government functions are 
performed even where a conflict of interest exists. "25 You have also provided no facts to suggest the 
"rule of necessity" would apply in the present situation. 
ER: jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
r-_
. 
By: 
Emelyn Rodriguez 
g 
Senior Counhel, Legal Division 
12 Section 109 l(a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005);83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000). 
u City of Vernon l'. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515;84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001). 
24 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 1 10 (2005). 
" Eldridge v. Sierra View Hospital Dist. ( 1990) 224 Cal. App. 3d 3 1 1 ,  322.

sections.question:
1. Does Superintendent Herrera's involvement in the City's Request For Proposal ("RFP") 
to outsource management of the City's golf course preclude him under Section 1090 from leaving 
city employment and seeking private employment with the selected company, CourseCo, to provide 
services he is currently performing as the City's Golf Course Superintendent? 
2. Under Section 1090, may Superintendent Herrera leave city employment and seek private 
employment as the Superintendent with the selected company, CourseCo, at a course not owned by 
the City of Santa Barbara?
sections.conclusion:
1. Yes. Superintendent Herrera cannot be hired by CourseCo as the City's Golf Course 
Superintendent without violating Section 1090. This is consistent with the City's representations to 
Superintendent Herrera and its agreement with CourseCo that he could not be hired in that position 
because he participated in making the contract. 
2. Yes, so long as he did not have an understanding, agreement, or arrangement regarding 
prospective employment with CourseCo at the time he participated in making the contract with the 
company.
sections.facts:

Mr. Sewell later clarified in an October 25, 2016 email that "the RFP document itself was silent on the

Attorney's office."

CourseCo and seeks an opinion from the FPPC as to whether or not his involvement in the RFP 
process would preclude him from becoming employed with CourseCo at the Golf Course, and 
whether such involvement would also preclude employment with CourseCo at any other golf course 
managed by CourseCo but not owned by the City.
sections.analysis:
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The purpose of Section 1090 is to 
ensure that "every public officer be guided solely by the public interest, rather than by personal 
interest, when dealing with contracts in an official capacity." 10 Eliminating temptation for public 
officials, avoiding the perception of impropriety, and obtaining public officials' undivided loyalty 
have been deemed extremely important public policy goals. Therefore, the prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. 11 A contract that 
violates Section 1090 is void.12 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. These steps include: (1) Does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) Does the decision involve a contract; (3) Is the official making or participating in 
making a contract; (4) Does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or 
non-interest exception apply. 
Steps one and two are not at issue here. Your facts indicate that Superintendent Herrera, as 
the City's Golf Course Superintendent is a city employee and the decision at issue involves a 
contract between the City and a private company, CourseCo. Therefore, we focus on the remaining 
steps, including whether Superintendent Herrera has participated in making the contract, whether he 
has a financial interest in the City's contract with CourseCo. to operate its municipal golf course, 
and whether a remote or non-interest exception applies. 
9 Mr. Herrera told the City Attorney in an October 19, 2016 email, "After the contract was awarded to 
CourseCo, one ofCourseCo's staff members inquired as to my availability, and my response was that I preferred to 
continue to work for the City." 
10 Thomson, .. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646. 
11 Id. at p. 648. 
12 Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.  
Steps 3 and 4: Did Superintendent Herrera make or participate in making a contract 
in which he had a financial interest? 
It is important to note that Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who actually execute 
contracts. Section 1090 casts a wide net to capture those officials who participate in any way in the 
making of the contract. 
The decisional law holds that an official (or a public employee) may be convicted of 
violation even if the official did not actually participate in the execution of the questioned contract, 
if it is established that he had the opportunity to, and did influence execution directly or indirectly 
to promote his personal interests.13 
Therefore, participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving 
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and 
specifications, and solicitation for bids.14 Individuals in advisory positions can influence the 
development of a contract during these early stages of the contracting process even though they 
have no actual power to execute the final contract.15 
Superintendent Herrera has been a long-time supervisor with the City who was assigned 
exclusively to the Golf Course as the Maintenance Supervisor. In that role, he was responsible for 
overseeing general maintenance of the Golf Course, including maintenance of the greens, driving 
range, clubhouse, parking lot, Golf Course equipment, purchasing materials, and overseeing 
renovation projects. 
Superintendent Herrera drafted the Maintenance Standards that were attached to the RFP. 
He was a member of the Evaluation Committee, took part in interviewing and selecting one of the 
five golf course management companies that made it through the initial selection process. 
Additionally, Mr. Herrera attended one negotiation meeting between the City and CourseCo. 
Based on these facts, we conclude that Superintendent Herrera participated in the contract 
between the City and CourseCo. to operate the City's Municipal Golf Course because he was 
involved in drafting Maintenance Standards that were incorporated in the contract, and he 
participated in preliminary discussions, negotiations, planning, solicitation for bids and the ranking 
of candidates. 
13 People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052. 
14 Millbrae Assn. for Residential Sun•im/ v. City of Millbrae ( 1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall 
v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569. 
15 See, e.g., Schaefer v. Berinstein, 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291 ;City Council v. McKinley ( 1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 
204 [member of Park and Recreation Board who owned a landscape architectural finn participated in the making of a 
contract in violation of Section 1090 where he was also a member of a committee created to advise the Board on the 
design, architecture, landscaping and technical planning of a Japanese garden]; see also 81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317, 318-
19 ( 1998) [after leaving office and establishing a private business, Section 1090 precluded former council member from 
participating in City's loan program where he had been involved in the "planning, discussions, and approval necessary 
to implement [the] loan program" and "had the opportunity and did participate in the policy decision 10 create the 
government program under which the contract would later be executed."  
The next issue is whether Superintendent Herrera had a financial interest in and stood to 
financially benefit from the contract between the City and CourseCo. 
Although Section l 090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law 
and Attorney General opinions indicate that the term is liberally interpreted. 16 Moreover, the 
certainty of financial gain is not necessa,y to create a conflict of interest. "The government's right 
to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is diminished as effectively where the 
officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he acts with certainty."17 
The prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve 
financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain.18 "Put in 
ordinary, but nonetheless precise, terms, an official has a financial interest in a contract if he might 
profit from it." (Eden Township Healthcare District v. Sutter Heath (201 1) 202 Cal.App.4th 208, at 
p. 221, quoting People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 at p. 333, italics in original.) 
In addition, courts and the Attorney General have stated that although an official or 
employee may resign from his or her position, that resignation may not be sufficient to avoid a 
Section 1090 violation when that person has been involved in the contracting process. For instance, 
county employees were advised that they could not propose an agreement for consultant services, 
then resign, and provide such consulting services. 19 
The Commission has also issued advice under Section 1090 regarding financial interests 
involving prospective employment. In the Culp Advice Letter, 1-14-051, we advised the Chief 
Deputy Director of a state agency that she had a financial interest in state contracts with various 
technology companies that she approved, if she had an agreement with, or had negotiated to provide 
future consulting services to the companies, or if certain clients of her consulting business were 
named as subcontractors or would otherwise receive benefits from the contracts. 
In Superintendent Herrera's situation, it is clear he participated in creating the contract that 
CourseCo. was ultimately selected to perform. However, at the time Superintendent Herrera 
participated in making the contract, the City made it clear to him and CourseCo. that he could not 
be hired as the City's Golf Course Superintendent. Therefore, he did not stand to financially benefit 
from his participation in the contract even though he created specifications for the RFP, and had the 
discretion to tailor requirements, including specific skill sets, and expertise of the ideal candidate. 
lf he is allowed to seek private employment with CourseCo as the City's Golf Course 
Superintendent, those facts would change the analysis and he would have had a financial interest in 
the contract in violation of Section 1090. 
16 People v. Deysher ( l 934) 2 Cal.2d l 41, 146 ["However devious and winding the chain may be which 
connects the officer with the forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void."] 
11 People ,,. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.41h 1271, 1298 (citations omitted). 
18 Thomson v. Call. supra, 38 Cal.3d 633, 651; see also People v. Va/lerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fu. 
5; Terry ,,. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-432; 85 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001). 
19 66 Ops. Ca.Atty.Gen. 156 (1983).  
This is the type of conduct Section 1090 attempts to thwart, and we recognize the enduring 
policies underlying Section I 090 that "no man can serve two masters," and that officials making 
contracts must not be distracted by personal financial gain but give absolute loyalty and undivided 
allegiance to the best interest of the agency they serve.20 
Given the specific facts in the matter before us, we believe the policies underlying Section 
1090 - eliminating temptation for public officials, avoiding the perception of impropriety, and 
obtaining public officials' undivided loyalty - were meant to prohibit this type of arrangement. 
Therefore, the prohibitions under Section 1090 apply because Superintendent Herrera had a 
financial interest in and stood to personally benefit from the contract in which he assisted the City 
in developing. 
We now tum to the question of whether Mr. Herrera could accept a position with CourseCo 
at a course not owned by the City of Santa Barbara, a position that is not specified in the actual 
contract in question. 
Your facts indicate that there was an understanding or agreement with CourseCo to retain 
city employees after being awarded the contract with the city. This was a factor in the winning bid. 
However, you state that the city had a verbal agreement with CourseCo that Mr. Herrera's 
employment was not part of this arrangement. 
Mr. Herrera also stated that he did not have any understanding, agreement, or arrangement 
with CourseCo regarding prospective employment at the time he participated in making the city's 
contract with CourseCo. He stated he was nevertheless approached by CourseCo staff about 
employment there but only "[a]fter the contract was awarded." 
Any arrangement or agreement made with CourseCo that would financially benefit Mr. 
Herrera at the time the city contract was made-including future employment at an another golf 
course-would be a violation of Section 1090 and Section 87407.21 
However, your facts state that there were no financial incentives or any separate 
arrangement regarding employment that was provided by CourseCo to Mr. Herrera at the time he 
participated in making the city's contract with CourseCo. 
Therefore, Mr. Herrera may pursue employment with CourseCo at a course not owned by 
the City of Santa Barbara so long as he did not have an understanding, agreement, or arrangement 
regarding prospective employment with CourseCo. 
20 See, e.g., Stigall v. Taft, supra, 58 Cal.2d 565, 569; City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 
19 l, l 96; City Council v. McKinley, supra, 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 212. 
11 Section 87407 states that "No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official 
position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom he or she is negotiating, or 
has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment," 
See also People v, Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal. App. 3d 847 [136 Cal. Rptr. 429). The Vallerga case demonstrates 
that Section I 090 applies even when a public official's financial interest flows from a source that is independent of a 
public contract.  
Step Five: Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply? 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where 
the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if ( 1) the officer in question 
discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest is noted 
in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of 
the contract.22 If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the officer's 
abstention, and generally, a noninterest does not require disclosure.23 You have provided no facts to 
suggest that any of the exceptions would apply to this situation. 
Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply? 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" applies to allow making a contract that 
Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit.24 Under the rule of necessity, a government agency may 
acquire an essential service, despite the existence of a conflict, when no source other than that 
which triggers the contract is available; the rule "ensures that essential government functions are 
performed even where a conflict of interest exists. "25 You have also provided no facts to suggest the 
"rule of necessity" would apply in the present situation. 
ER: jgl

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does Superintendent Herrera's involvement in the City's Request For Proposal ("RFP") 
to outsource management of the City's golf course preclude him under Section 1090 from leaving 
city employment and seeking private employment with the selected company, CourseCo, to provide 
services he is currently performing as the City's Golf Course Superintendent? 
2. Under Section 1090, may Superintendent Herrera leave city employment and seek private 
employment as the Superintendent with the selected company, CourseCo, at a course not owned by 
the City of Santa Barbara?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. Superintendent Herrera cannot be hired by CourseCo as the City's Golf Course 
Superintendent without violating Section 1090. This is consistent with the City's representations to 
Superintendent Herrera and its agreement with CourseCo that he could not be hired in that position 
because he participated in making the contract. 
2. Yes, so long as he did not have an understanding, agreement, or arrangement regarding 
prospective employment with CourseCo at the time he participated in making the contract with the 
company.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Tava Ostrenger Assistant City Attorney City of Santa Barbara PO Box 1990 Santa Barbara, CA 93102 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-088 Dear Ms. Ostrenger: January 6, 2017 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Barbara City Golf Course Superintendent Simon Herrera regarding Government Code Section l 090, et seq. 1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area oflaw, other than Section l 090 and the Political Reform Act (the "Act").2 We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice, and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.3 Moreover, the Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct.4 Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. As required by statute, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Barbara County District Attorney's Office and we did not receive a written response from either entity.5 Finally, we are required to advise you that the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. 6 1 Government Code Sections l090 through 1097.5. All statutory references arc to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. 2 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9!014. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code ofRegulatiqns, unless otherwise indicated. 3 In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71. 4 Section 109 l.7(c)(2). s Section 1097. l(c)(3) and (4). 6 Section 1097.l(c)(S). QUESTIONS File No. A-16-088 Page No. 2 1. Does Superintendent Herrera's involvement in the City's Request For Proposal ("RFP") to outsource management of the City's golf course preclude him under Section 1090 from leaving city employment and seeking private employment with the selected company, CourseCo, to provide services he is currently performing as the City's Golf Course Superintendent? 2. Under Section 1090, may Superintendent Herrera leave city employment and seek private employment as the Superintendent with the selected company, CourseCo, at a course not owned by the City of Santa Barbara? CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. Superintendent Herrera cannot be hired by CourseCo as the City's Golf Course Superintendent without violating Section 1090. This is consistent with the City's representations to Superintendent Herrera and its agreement with CourseCo that he could not be hired in that position because he participated in making the contract. 2. Yes, so long as he did not have an understanding, agreement, or arrangement regarding prospective employment with CourseCo at the time he participated in making the contract with the company. FACTS The
